items.question: State Bayes' theorem and name each factor in an inference problem.
items.answer: P(A|B) = P(B|A)P(A)/P(B). P(A) is the prior, P(B|A) the likelihood, P(B) the evidence (marginal), and P(A|B) the posterior. Requires P(B) > 0.

items.question: A test has 99% sensitivity and 95% specificity; disease prevalence is 0.1%. A patient tests positive. What is P(disease | positive)?
items.answer: P(+) = 0.99×0.001 + 0.05×0.999 ≈ 0.0509. P(disease|+) = 0.99×0.001/0.0509 ≈ 0.019 (about 1.9%). The rare prior keeps the posterior low despite good test accuracy.

items.question: An interviewer asks: what is the prosecutor's fallacy and how does Bayes' theorem fix it?
items.answer: The prosecutor's fallacy equates P(evidence|suspect guilty) with P(suspect guilty|evidence). Bayes' theorem requires multiplying by the prior P(guilty) and dividing by P(evidence) to get the correct posterior. Rare guilt probability means strong forensic match still may not imply high guilt probability.

items.question: How does Naive Bayes use Bayes' theorem at prediction time?
items.answer: For class c and features x, predict argmax_c P(c|x) ∝ P(x|c)P(c). Bayes' theorem converts the classification question to prior P(c) times likelihood P(x|c); Naive Bayes factorizes P(x|c) for tractability. Normalization over classes uses P(x) = Σ_c P(x|c)P(c).

items.question: Why can P(B|A) not be substituted directly when you need P(A|B)?
items.answer: P(B|A) answers how likely evidence is under hypothesis A; P(A|B) answers how likely A is given observed B. They differ unless P(A)=P(B). Bayes' theorem weights P(B|A) by prior P(A) and normalizes by P(B) to perform the reversal.
